```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
diplomat("Gilbert"; "Macdonald"; "Pickett"; "Vasquez").
month(1; 2; 3; 4).
duration(4; 5; 9; 10).

% Each diplomat is assigned exactly one month and one duration.
assign(D, M, Dur) :- diplomat(D), month(M), duration(Dur), #count { D2, M2, Dur2 : assign(D2, M2, Dur2) } = 1, D2 = D.

% Each month is used exactly once.
:- #count { D, Dur : assign(D, M, Dur) } != 1, month(M).

% Each duration is used exactly once.
:- #count { D, M : assign(D, M, Dur) } != 1, duration(Dur).

% 1. Vasquez leaves after Macdonald.
:- assign("Vasquez", M1, _), assign("Macdonald", M2, _), M1 <= M2.

% 2. Vasquez leaves 1 month before the ambassador with the 5-day visit.
:- assign("Vasquez", M1, _), assign(_, M2, 5), M1 != M2 - 1.

% 3.1 Gilbert and the ambassador with the 4-day visit are different.
:- assign("Gilbert", _, 4).

% 3.2 Gilbert is the person leaving in January OR the ambassador with the 4-day visit.
:- assign("Gilbert", M, Dur), M != 1, Dur != 4.

% 3.3 The ambassador with the 4-day visit is the person leaving in January OR the ambassador with the 4-day visit.
:- assign(D, M, 4), M != 1, Dur != 4.  % Note: Dur is 4 here, so this is redundant; better to rephrase

% Correcting 3.3: The ambassador with 4-day visit is in January OR is the 4-day visit (tautology) — so just ensure that if Dur=4, then M=1 OR Dur=4 — which is always true.
% So the constraint is really: the 4-day visit must be in January OR the ambassador with 4-day visit is the one in January — i.e., if Dur=4, then M=1.
:- assign(_, M, 4), M != 1.

% 4. Macdonald leaves 1 month before the ambassador with the 4-day visit.
:- assign("Macdonald", M1, _), assign(_, M2, 4), M1 != M2 - 1.

% 5. The ambassador with the 4-day visit leaves before the ambassador with the 9-day visit.
:- assign(_, M1, 4), assign(_, M2, 9), M1 >= M2.
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
names("Cornick"; "Dreadco"; "Foltron"; "Worul").
months(1; 2; 3; 4).
companies("Permias"; "Rubicorp"; "Techtrin"; "Ubersplore").

% Each company launches exactly one rocket in exactly one month.
% Use choice rules with cardinality constraints to ensure one-to-one mapping.
% For each company, choose one rocket and one month.
{launches(C, N, M)} = 1 :- companies(C), names(N), months(M).

% Each rocket is launched by exactly one company.
{launches(C, N, M)} = 1 :- names(N), months(M), companies(C).

% Each month has exactly one launch.
{launches(C, N, M)} = 1 :- companies(C), names(N), months(M).

% Constraint 1: The Worul, the rocket that launches in February, and the rocket that launches in January are all different rockets.
% That is: the rocket named "Worul" ≠ the rocket launched in January ≠ the rocket launched in February.
% So: if a rocket is launched in January, it's not Worul; if launched in February, it's not Worul; and January and February rockets are different.
:- launches(_, "Worul", 1).
:- launches(_, "Worul", 2).
:- launches(_, N1, 1), launches(_, N2, 2), N1 == N2.

% Constraint 2: The Dreadco is made by Ubersplore.
:- launches(C, "Dreadco", M), C != "Ubersplore".

% Constraint 3: The rocket developed by Permias will launch 1 month before the Foltron.
:- launches("Permias", _, M1), launches(_, "Foltron", M2), M1 != M2 - 1.

% Constraint 4: The rocket that will launch in January is made by Techtrin.
:- launches(C, _, 1), C != "Techtrin".

% Ensure all assignments are unique (enforced by the above constraints and cardinality).
% We rely on the cardinality constraints and uniqueness of values to avoid duplicates.
% The above constraints already enforce that each company, rocket, and month is used exactly once.
```
